title: Did 3I really alter reality
wikitext:
[]: Did 3I really "alter" reality?             Quote Post by KingXanaduu » Tue Apr 09, 2013 9:31 pm I've done a lot of research and reading on a multitude of forums, including this one, on what exactly the hell went on in Third Impact. While a lot of source and discussions provided a ton of insight of what may have went on, a lot of them also state that the "fabric of reality" was "warped" and "altered". Now, I can understand where this insight may come from, considering the scale of the event, but was it really on a scale that altered "reality"? From what I can infer, Third Impact's influence was massive in its scale, compared to us, considering it altered the fabric of life on earth. But in actuality, this range only seemed to extend on Earth and the Moon (considering the blood stain on it). This makes the event "world-shattering", but is that really the same thing as "altering reality"? Now, one could argue that altering the fabric of life and death by gathering everyone's soul to join together in a Black Moon of a hive mind is an example. However, considering in this universe how people's souls are as malleable as Silly Putty and how easy it is to cause these Impacts if two simple forces join together, this isn't actually saying much. From what I can tell, this event only simply too place on Earth and altered the fabric of life on it, but it doesn't actually go much farther than that to warrant a "reality warping" definition. The Sun is still burning, the planets are still orbiting, the basic laws of physics are still in play as far as I can see. Yes, Earth is in ruin and humanity is now a giant puddle of amino acids, but is it really that massive in scale to the rest of the universe? Compared to the rest of the universe, consider how we only know just about a tiny fraction of its size, Third Impact really isn't as big as it seems. There are probably billions of civilizations that exist in our galaxy alone, and they wouldn't even consider what's happening. The entire earth could scream in a torrent of ecstasy and pain as billions burst into blobs of proto-life...and the rest of the universe wouldn't even notice.

[]:             Quote Post by Lavinius » Tue Apr 09, 2013 10:09 pm In some philosophical systems (such as George Berkeley's ) reality is defined and exists only by the minds of the people perceiving it. Especially in the aired ending, this sort of idea is promoted; so that's probably what it's referring to.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Tue Apr 09, 2013 11:32 pm It is true that Instrumentality doesn't seem to affect the entire universe using EoE as evidence, but for the scope of the story it doesn't really make any difference.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Apr 10, 2013 12:03 am EoE doesn't alter anything, since from the perspective of the newly created Godhead, there is no before and after and nothing is ever changed. Maaaaan.

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Wed Apr 10, 2013 6:28 am Third Impact was never an event which significantly affected the material universe. Third Impact was primarily about absorbing all souls on earth into Lilith's egg, to be reborn as a single being. There's also no suggestion that 3I was anything other than a localised event, earth-wise. We know "Impacts" can be limited in range/power because of Second Impacts limited range after the Katsurugi team (presumably) got Adam to use up enough of its AATF energy beforehand.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Wed Apr 10, 2013 7:45 am I think that the theory that Third Impact altered reality come from the various lines during Instrumentality talking about "one of the possibilities", or "this is one of the many outcomes", and of course the Alternate Universe scene in EoTV : it was never made clear if it was just an illusion created by Instrumentality for Shinji, or if it really was an Alternate Universe that Instrumentality let him observe temporarily. And the fact almost every "alternate stories" of Evangelion(videogames, mangas like Campus Paradise...) more or less heavily implies that there is indeed a system of Multiverses and that some characters have traveled from an universe to another(Kaworu in the vast majority of cases) doesn't really helps matters. And let's not start on Rebuild... As for the scale, even if in the scale of the Universe Third Impact on Earth means nothing, that didn't means that the fabric of reality hadn't been altered : it's possible that reality was altered on a "local" scale : TI "tore up" Earth from the fabric of reality, altered it and then "patched it back" and voilà ! If reality really has been fooled with, the big question would be : did TI altered NGE's Earth reality, or did it merely opened a gate(albeit temporary) to other realities?

[]:             Quote Post by TehDonutKing » Wed Apr 10, 2013 10:19 am Kaworu being a slider is pretty much limited to the Super Robot Wars series, so i doubt that it applies to the series.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Apr 10, 2013 10:57 am TehDonutKing wrote: Kaworu being a slider is pretty much limited to the Super Robot Wars series, so i doubt that it applies to the series.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Wed Apr 10, 2013 1:13 pm Even if Third Impact was a reality-altering event, it could've just focused the local reality. Nothing about reality-warping demands universal repercussions.

[]:             Quote Post by Reichu » Wed Apr 10, 2013 3:36 pm Lavinius wrote: In some philosophical systems (such as George Berkeley's ) reality is defined and exists only by the minds of the people perceiving it. Especially in the aired ending, this sort of idea is promoted; so that's probably what it's referring to.

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Wed Apr 10, 2013 5:24 pm The talk of worlds in the Instrumentality of the TV ending I always took in the sense of each individual's Cartesian Theater playing to their own brain in a jar (or, rather, quantum brain emulation in LCL); with there being -- by intent -- increasing levels of leakage between the disparate shows playing in each. While there are definite similarities here with the participant subjective experiences in the later stages of Bear's Blood Music (with characters being computed in the junk DNA of single cells), the scenario in NGE doesn't reach the quantum-observer supersaturation that caused problems in objective reality outside of that story's LCL sea-equivalent.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Apr 11, 2013 3:43 am Said simply : it was all in their head, right?

[]:             Quote Post by ObsessiveMathsFreak » Thu Apr 11, 2013 2:11 pm Apart from when their bodies exploded into LCL of course.

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Thu Apr 11, 2013 2:44 pm Of course! But still, it was never really clear if the AU of episode 26 is supposed to be all in Shinji's head or truly an Alternate Universe... the fact they made a manga out of it doesn't help to clarify matters.

[]:             Quote Post by BC Baron » Thu Apr 11, 2013 4:42 pm From what I can recall, the only observable ways in which the Second Impact had an influence on the physical world were that the Antarctic explosion melted the polar ice caps and caused a slight shift in the tilt of the planet's axis. According to Class 2-A's teacher, this was responsible for the climate change that altered Japan's weather patterns, creating a perpetual summer. While it's possible that Third Impact might have brought about comparable mutations to the environment of the external world, I suspect the majority of the heavy-duty, earth-shattering side-effects took place mostly in the hearts and minds of the characters (as well as the audience).

[]:             Quote Post by Mr. Tines » Thu Apr 11, 2013 4:59 pm ElMariachi wrote: it was never really clear if the AU of episode 26 is supposed to be all in Shinji's head or truly an Alternate Universe

[]:             Quote Post by ElMariachi » Fri Apr 12, 2013 2:49 am Mr. Tines wrote: Depending on what model of reality you are working on, the answer could be "all of the above" (I'm thinking of the abstract configuration space "Dust" model from Egan's Permutation City here). On a more prosaic level, having a brief didactic sensory impression of a physically plausible universe says nothing of the existence of such a scene in the baseline reality of a Tegmark Level 1 multiverse, let alone anything more exotic.

[]:             Quote Post by AuraTwilight » Fri Apr 12, 2013 10:52 am No, that's literally how Tines speaks all the time, basically.
